---
title: "Chapter 05 — 📈 Linear & Logistic Models"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 04 · 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering](ch04.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [06 · 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers →](ch06.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Linear and logistic models are the workhorses of classical machine learning: a prediction is just a weighted sum of the inputs, optionally squashed through a simple function. They are fast, interpretable, and surprisingly hard to beat on tabular data, which is why they remain the default first model and the baseline every fancier method must justify itself against. This chapter sits at the entrance to classical ML — once you understand a line through data and the probability twist that turns it into a classifier, most of the rest of the field is variations on the theme.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Classical supervised learning · used for prediction (regression) and classification on tabular data · the one key idea: a model that is *linear in its parameters* is easy to fit, easy to read, and the honest baseline for everything else.
:::

## 5.1 — Linear regression: fitting a line to data

The intuition is the one you met in school: draw the straight line that passes "through the middle" of a cloud of points. **Linear regression** formalizes this. Given inputs $x_1, \dots, x_d$ (the **features**) we predict a continuous target $y$ as a weighted sum plus an offset:

$$ \hat{y} = w_0 + w_1 x_1 + \cdots + w_d x_d = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} $$

where $w_0$ is the **intercept** (or bias) and we fold it in by prepending a constant $1$ to every input vector. The weights $\mathbf{w}$ are what we learn.

What does "through the middle" mean precisely? We pick the weights that minimize the **sum of squared errors** between predictions and truth — the **least squares** criterion:

$$ J(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i \right)^2 = \lVert \mathbf{y} - X\mathbf{w} \rVert^2 $$

We square the errors so that positive and negative misses do not cancel, and so that large misses are penalized disproportionately. Squaring also makes the objective smooth and convex, which means there is a single global minimum and no local traps.

Geometrically, least squares has a clean picture: the predictions $X\mathbf{w}$ live in the subspace spanned by the feature columns, and the best fit is the **orthogonal projection** of the target $\mathbf{y}$ onto that subspace. The residual — the leftover error — sticks out at a right angle to every feature, which is exactly the condition the normal equation below encodes.

<svg viewBox="0 0 260 150" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <polygon points="20,120 200,120 240,90 60,90" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.12" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="70" y1="110" x2="160" y2="30" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="70" y1="110" x2="150" y2="103" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="150" y1="103" x2="160" y2="30" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/>
  <rect x="143" y="96" width="7" height="7" fill="none" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <circle cx="70" cy="110" r="2.5" fill="currentColor"/>
  <circle cx="160" cy="30" r="2.5" fill="currentColor"/>
  <text x="162" y="28" font-size="9" fill="currentColor">y</text>
  <text x="120" y="115" font-size="9" fill="#22c55e">ŷ = Xw</text>
  <text x="163" y="70" font-size="9" fill="#ef4444">residual</text>
  <text x="205" y="105" font-size="8" fill="#3b82f6">feature space</text>
</svg>

There are two ways to find that minimum.

**The normal equation** solves it in one shot. Setting the gradient to zero gives a closed-form answer:

$$ \mathbf{w} = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top \mathbf{y} $$

This is exact and needs no tuning, but it inverts a $d \times d$ matrix — roughly $O(d^3)$ work — so it becomes painful when you have many features, and it breaks down when $X^\top X$ is not invertible (e.g. duplicated or perfectly correlated columns).

**Gradient descent** instead walks downhill. The gradient of $J$ is $-2 X^\top(\mathbf{y} - X\mathbf{w})$; we repeatedly step against it. This scales to huge datasets (especially in its stochastic form, see Chapter 15) and handles the non-invertible case gracefully, at the cost of choosing a learning rate and iterating.

```python
import numpy as np

def fit_normal(X, y):              # closed form
    Xb = np.c_[np.ones(len(X)), X] # prepend bias column
    return np.linalg.solve(Xb.T @ Xb, Xb.T @ y)  # solve, don't invert

def fit_gd(X, y, lr=0.01, epochs=2000):
    Xb = np.c_[np.ones(len(X)), X]
    w = np.zeros(Xb.shape[1])
    for _ in range(epochs):
        grad = -2 * Xb.T @ (y - Xb @ w) / len(X)  # mean gradient
        w -= lr * grad
    return w
```

Use `np.linalg.solve`, never `np.linalg.inv` — solving the system is more numerically stable and faster than forming the explicit inverse.

## 5.2 — What the coefficients mean, and how good is the fit

A fitted linear model is a story you can read off the weights. Each coefficient $w_j$ answers a precise counterfactual: **holding all other features fixed, a one-unit increase in $x_j$ changes the prediction by $w_j$.** That "holding others fixed" clause is the whole subtlety — coefficients are *partial* effects, not raw correlations, so a feature's weight depends on what else is in the model.

A few practical consequences:

- **Units matter.** A weight of $0.5$ on "income in dollars" and $5000$ on "income in thousands" describe the same relationship. To compare feature importances by coefficient size, first standardize features to zero mean and unit variance (see Chapter 04).
- **Sign is the direction** of the effect; magnitude (on standardized inputs) is the strength.
- **Correlated features split the credit** unpredictably between themselves, which is one reason large coefficients are not automatically "important."

To judge the fit as a whole, the standard summary is **$R^2$**, the coefficient of determination — the fraction of the target's variance the model explains:

$$ R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_i (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_i (y_i - \bar{y})^2} $$

The denominator is the error of the dumbest model (always predict the mean $\bar{y}$); the numerator is your model's error. So $R^2 = 1$ is perfect, $R^2 = 0$ means "no better than the mean," and negative values are possible on test data when a model is genuinely worse than guessing the mean.

::: {.callout-warning}
$R^2$ never decreases when you add features, even random noise ones — so a high $R^2$ on training data is not evidence of a good model. Use **adjusted $R^2$**, which penalizes extra parameters, or better, report $R^2$ on held-out data (Chapter 11).
:::

Ordinary least squares also rests on **assumptions** that govern when its coefficients and uncertainty estimates are trustworthy: a roughly linear relationship, errors that are independent, have constant variance (**homoscedasticity**), and are not too far from normal, plus features that are not perfectly collinear. Violations do not necessarily ruin prediction, but they do undermine the interpretation and the confidence intervals. When two or more features are strongly correlated — **multicollinearity** — the model cannot tell their effects apart, so the individual coefficients swing wildly from sample to sample even though the overall prediction stays stable. This is exactly the instability that ridge regression (Section 5.4) tames.

## 5.3 — Beyond straight lines: polynomial and basis-function regression

"Linear" sounds limiting — what if the relationship curves? The key realization is that **linear regression is linear in the *parameters*, not in the inputs.** Nothing stops us from feeding it transformed features. If we add $x^2, x^3, \dots$ as new columns, we get **polynomial regression**, which fits curves while remaining an ordinary least-squares problem in disguise.

More generally we replace each input $x$ with a set of **basis functions** $\phi_1(x), \phi_2(x), \dots$ and fit:

$$ \hat{y} = \sum_{j} w_j\, \phi_j(x) $$

The $\phi_j$ can be polynomials, sines and cosines (a Fourier basis), Gaussian bumps (**radial basis functions**), or splines. The model stays linear and convex in $\mathbf{w}$; we have simply enriched the feature space.

```python
# Degree-3 polynomial features for a single input column x
def poly_features(x, degree=3):
    return np.vstack([x**p for p in range(1, degree+1)]).T  # cols: x, x^2, x^3
```

The tradeoff is the central tension of all of ML: too few basis functions and the model **underfits** (too rigid to capture the shape); too many and it **overfits** (it wiggles to chase noise and generalizes badly). A degree-15 polynomial can pass exactly through every training point and be useless on new data. This rigidity-versus-wiggle balance is the **bias–variance tradeoff**, treated in full in Chapter 11.

<svg viewBox="0 0 320 120" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <line x1="20" y1="100" x2="300" y2="100" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/>
  <circle cx="50" cy="70" r="2.5" fill="currentColor"/><circle cx="90" cy="55" r="2.5" fill="currentColor"/>
  <circle cx="130" cy="60" r="2.5" fill="currentColor"/><circle cx="170" cy="40" r="2.5" fill="currentColor"/>
  <circle cx="210" cy="48" r="2.5" fill="currentColor"/><circle cx="250" cy="30" r="2.5" fill="currentColor"/>
  <path d="M40 78 L260 28" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="2" fill="none"/>
  <path d="M40 80 C90 30, 110 90, 170 38 S230 70, 260 25" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none"/>
  <text x="262" y="22" font-size="9" fill="#3b82f6">good fit</text>
  <text x="120" y="105" font-size="9" fill="#ef4444">overfit wiggle</text>
</svg>

The cure for overfitting is not "use fewer features" by hand — it is **regularization**, the subject of the next section.

## 5.4 — Regularization: ridge, lasso, and elastic net

The lazy and powerful way to control model complexity is to keep all your features but discourage the weights from getting large. The intuition: a wildly wiggling overfit curve needs big, finely balanced coefficients; if we make big coefficients "expensive," the model prefers smoother solutions. We do this by adding a **penalty** term to the loss.

**Ridge regression** (L2) penalizes the sum of squared weights:

$$ J(\mathbf{w}) = \lVert \mathbf{y} - X\mathbf{w} \rVert^2 + \alpha \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2^2 $$

The strength $\alpha \ge 0$ is a **hyperparameter** you tune by cross-validation (Chapter 11). Ridge shrinks all coefficients toward zero smoothly but never exactly to zero. It has a clean closed form, $\mathbf{w} = (X^\top X + \alpha I)^{-1} X^\top \mathbf{y}$, and the added $\alpha I$ conveniently makes the matrix invertible again — solving the collinearity problem from 5.1.

**Lasso** (L1) penalizes the sum of *absolute* weights:

$$ J(\mathbf{w}) = \lVert \mathbf{y} - X\mathbf{w} \rVert^2 + \alpha \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_1 $$

This one small change has a dramatic effect: lasso drives some coefficients **exactly to zero**, performing automatic **feature selection**. The geometric reason is that the L1 constraint region is a diamond with sharp corners on the axes, and the loss contours tend to first touch it at a corner — where some weights are zero. Ridge's constraint region is a smooth circle, so it has no such corners.

<svg viewBox="0 0 280 120" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <g transform="translate(70,60)">
    <line x1="-50" y1="0" x2="50" y2="0" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="0.5"/>
    <line x1="0" y1="-50" x2="0" y2="50" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="0.5"/>
    <polygon points="0,-30 30,0 0,30 -30,0" fill="none" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <ellipse cx="25" cy="-22" rx="28" ry="18" fill="none" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="1"/>
    <circle cx="0" cy="-30" r="3" fill="#ef4444"/>
    <text x="-20" y="48" font-size="9" fill="#ef4444">L1 (lasso)</text>
  </g>
  <g transform="translate(200,60)">
    <line x1="-50" y1="0" x2="50" y2="0" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="0.5"/>
    <line x1="0" y1="-50" x2="0" y2="50" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="0.5"/>
    <circle cx="0" cy="0" r="30" fill="none" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <ellipse cx="25" cy="-22" rx="28" ry="18" fill="none" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="1"/>
    <circle cx="18" cy="-24" r="3" fill="#22c55e"/>
    <text x="-22" y="48" font-size="9" fill="#22c55e">L2 (ridge)</text>
  </g>
</svg>

**Elastic net** simply blends both penalties, $\alpha_1 \lVert \mathbf{w}\rVert_1 + \alpha_2 \lVert \mathbf{w}\rVert_2^2$, getting lasso's sparsity while behaving more stably when features are correlated (where pure lasso tends to arbitrarily pick one of a correlated group and zero the rest).

There is a deeper way to see all three: regularization is just **maximum-likelihood fitting with a prior belief that weights are small.** Ridge corresponds to a Gaussian prior on the weights, lasso to a Laplace (double-exponential) prior whose sharp peak at zero is what produces sparsity. That Bayesian reading is developed in Chapter 12; for now the takeaway is that the penalty encodes a prior preference for simpler models.

| Method | Penalty | Effect on weights | Best when |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ridge | $\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert_2^2$ | shrinks all, none exactly 0 | many small effects; correlated features |
| Lasso | $\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert_1$ | sets some exactly to 0 | few features truly matter; want selection |
| Elastic net | both | sparse, but groups stay together | many correlated, want selection too |

::: {.callout-tip}
Always **standardize features before regularizing.** The penalty acts on coefficient magnitudes, so if one feature is in dollars and another in millions, the penalty hits them unequally for no good reason. And never penalize the intercept — it just sets the overall level.
:::

## 5.5 — Logistic regression: turning a line into a classifier

Now suppose the target is a yes/no label, not a number. We could try to predict $0$ or $1$ directly with a line, but a line happily outputs $-3.2$ or $4.7$, which are not probabilities. **Logistic regression** fixes this by computing the same linear score and then squashing it into the $[0,1]$ range with the **sigmoid** (logistic) function:

$$ \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \qquad \hat{p} = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}) = P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}) $$

The sigmoid is an S-curve: very negative scores map near $0$, very positive near $1$, and a score of $0$ maps to exactly $0.5$.

<svg viewBox="0 0 240 120" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <line x1="20" y1="100" x2="220" y2="100" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <line x1="120" y1="20" x2="120" y2="108" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="0.8"/>
  <line x1="20" y1="60" x2="220" y2="60" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="0.4" stroke-dasharray="3 3"/>
  <path d="M20 98 C70 96, 100 90, 120 60 C140 30, 170 24, 220 22" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="2" fill="none"/>
  <circle cx="120" cy="60" r="3" fill="#ef4444"/>
  <text x="124" y="54" font-size="9" fill="#ef4444">0.5</text>
  <text x="222" y="25" font-size="8" fill="currentColor">1</text>
  <text x="108" y="118" font-size="8" fill="currentColor">z = wᵀx</text>
</svg>

That $0.5$ point is the **decision boundary** — and because $\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x} = 0$ is a straight line (or hyperplane), logistic regression is a **linear classifier** even though its output curve is not linear.

The linear score has a beautiful interpretation. Inverting the sigmoid shows that the model is linear in the **log-odds**:

$$ \log \frac{\hat{p}}{1-\hat{p}} = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} $$

So each weight $w_j$ is the change in **log-odds** per unit of $x_j$, and $e^{w_j}$ is the **odds ratio** — multiply the odds by that factor for each unit increase. This is exactly why logistic regression is the standard tool in medicine and credit scoring: regulators and doctors can read the coefficients.

How do we fit it? Squared error is the wrong loss here (it makes the problem non-convex and punishes confident-correct predictions oddly). Instead we use **maximum likelihood**: choose weights that make the observed labels most probable. Taking the negative log gives the convex **cross-entropy** (log) loss:

$$ J(\mathbf{w}) = -\sum_i \Big[ y_i \log \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{p}_i) \Big] $$

There is no closed form, but the loss is convex, and — pleasingly — its gradient has the *exact same shape* as linear regression's: $X^\top(\hat{\mathbf{p}} - \mathbf{y})$. The only change from 5.1 is the sigmoid wrapped around the prediction.

```python
def sigmoid(z): return 1 / (1 + np.exp(-z))

def fit_logistic(X, y, lr=0.1, epochs=3000):
    Xb = np.c_[np.ones(len(X)), X]
    w = np.zeros(Xb.shape[1])
    for _ in range(epochs):
        p = sigmoid(Xb @ w)
        grad = Xb.T @ (p - y) / len(X)   # same form as linear regression
        w -= lr * grad
    return w
# predict label: sigmoid(Xb @ w) >= 0.5
```

Logistic regression can overfit too — especially with many features or perfectly separable classes, where the weights run off to infinity chasing ever-more-confident predictions. The same L2 or L1 penalty from Section 5.4 is the standard fix and is switched on by default in most libraries.

::: {.callout-warning}
The default $0.5$ threshold is a convention, not a law. On imbalanced data (say 2% fraud) it can label everything negative and still score well on accuracy. Pick the threshold from the precision/recall tradeoff your application needs, and judge the model with ROC-AUC or precision-recall, not raw accuracy (Chapter 11).
:::

## 5.6 — More than two classes: softmax regression

When there are $K$ classes instead of two, we generalize the sigmoid to the **softmax** function. The idea: compute one linear score $z_k = \mathbf{w}_k^\top \mathbf{x}$ per class, then normalize the scores into a probability distribution that sums to one:

$$ P(y = k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}} $$

This is **multinomial (softmax) logistic regression**. Exponentiating makes every score positive; dividing by the total makes them sum to one. The exponential also means the largest score dominates, "softly" picking a winner — hence the name. With $K=2$, softmax collapses back to the ordinary sigmoid, so logistic regression is just its two-class special case.

The loss is again cross-entropy, now summed over classes, and it trains the same way by gradient descent. The same softmax layer reappears, unchanged, as the final layer of nearly every neural-network classifier (Chapter 14) — classical and deep learning meet here.

::: {.callout-tip}
Two ways to do multiclass: train one true softmax model (couples the classes, gives a proper joint distribution), or train $K$ separate one-vs-rest binary classifiers (simpler, parallelizable, but probabilities need renormalizing). Softmax is usually the cleaner default.
:::

## 5.7 — The unifying view: generalized linear models

Linear, logistic, and softmax regression look like three tools, but they are three instances of one framework: the **generalized linear model (GLM)**. Every GLM has the same three parts:

1. A **linear predictor** $\eta = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}$ — the weighted sum, always.
2. A **link function** $g$ that connects the linear predictor to the mean of the target: $g(\mathbb{E}[y]) = \eta$. Its inverse maps the score back to the prediction's natural range.
3. A **distribution** for the target from the exponential family (Gaussian, Bernoulli, Poisson, …).

Seen this way, the models differ only in their choice of link and distribution:

| Target type | Distribution | Inverse-link (score → prediction) | Model |
|---|---|---|---|
| Continuous | Gaussian | identity | Linear regression |
| Binary | Bernoulli | sigmoid | Logistic regression |
| One of $K$ | Categorical | softmax | Softmax regression |
| Counts ($0,1,2,\dots$) | Poisson | exponential | Poisson regression |

The payoff of the GLM view is conceptual economy: once you can fit one of these by maximum likelihood and gradient descent, you can fit all of them by swapping the link and the loss. **Poisson regression** for count data (website visits, defects per batch) drops out for free, with $\log \mathbb{E}[y] = \mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}$. It is also the bridge to deep learning, where a network is essentially a stack of nonlinear feature transforms feeding a GLM-style output layer.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  X["features x"] --> L["linear predictor<br/>η = wᵀx"]
  L --> G["inverse link g⁻¹"]
  G --> Y["prediction E[y]"]
  G -. identity .-> R["regression"]
  G -. sigmoid .-> B["binary class"]
  G -. softmax .-> M["multiclass"]
  G -. exp .-> P["counts"]
```

## 5.8 — Key takeaways

- Linear regression fits $\hat{y}=\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}$ by least squares; solve it exactly with the normal equation (small $d$) or iteratively with gradient descent (large data). Geometrically, the fit is the orthogonal projection of $\mathbf{y}$ onto the feature space.
- A coefficient is the effect of one feature *holding the others fixed*; standardize before comparing magnitudes, and judge overall fit with $R^2$ on **held-out** data.
- Linear models are linear in the *parameters* — polynomial and basis-function features let them fit curves while staying a convex least-squares problem, trading off underfitting against overfitting (bias–variance).
- Regularization controls overfitting: **ridge** (L2) shrinks smoothly and cures multicollinearity, **lasso** (L1) zeroes out features for selection, **elastic net** blends both. Always standardize first; the penalties are equivalent to priors on the weights.
- Logistic regression squashes the linear score through a **sigmoid** to get a probability; it is linear in the **log-odds**, has a linear decision boundary, and is fit by maximizing likelihood (cross-entropy loss).
- **Softmax** regression extends this to $K$ classes; the same layer is the output of most neural classifiers.
- **GLMs** unify all of these as (linear predictor + link function + distribution) — change the link and the loss to get a new model for free.

## 5.9 — See also

- **Chapter 02 — Mathematics for Machine Learning** (linear algebra and the gradients behind these formulas)
- **Chapter 04 — Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering** (standardization, basis features, encoding)
- **Chapter 11 — Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection** (cross-validation, bias–variance, $R^2$, ROC-AUC, choosing $\alpha$ and thresholds)
- **Chapter 07 — Support Vector Machines & Kernels** (another linear classifier, with a different margin-based loss and the kernel trick)
- **Chapter 12 — Bayesian Machine Learning** (regularization as a prior; Bayesian linear regression)
- **Chapter 15 — Training Deep Neural Networks** (stochastic gradient descent and optimization at scale)
- **Chapter 14 — Neural Network Fundamentals** (where the softmax output layer reappears)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 04 · 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering](ch04.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [06 · 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers →](ch06.html)
